reviews.review_text:
I have played this game long enough to know:

Cons
- It will destroy everything you know, all that you've loved and owned

Pros
- You will have the most WW2 trivia knowledge in the entire homeless shelter
- The game's pretty fun

More pros than cons so you should play this game for a thousand hours and still not know how the navy works
This is an absolute masterpiece of a game. Probably my favorite game, yet i am giving it a negative review because paradox is an incredibly greedy company that only cares about money.
absolutely bloody brilliant lad! everything about this game was just mint! my favourite moment was the douglass mcarthur laser penis sex battle, that was brilliant
Truly one of the most magnificent games ever, a game has never made me so angry while still keeping me adicted at the same time
Hearts of Iron IV is the greatest WW2 strategy game ever created. What else needs to be said!?

only game where even after 1k+ hours, you have very little clue of what's actually happening
in other words, buy it
My friend keeps insisting on femboy Poland mods. either way, i love this game, my type of strategy game

I love this game, but I cannot recommend buying it because of Paradox's DLC policy. 

The base game is negatively affected by the amount of mechanics locked behind DLC paywalls, and it ends up feeling like a more and more empty shell.
The devs have to try to juggle between keeping the base game functional while allowing the DLCs to drastically change core mechanics or add entirely new ones. It doesn't work well a lot of the time, and that means the base game has been degraded over the years.

The only scenario where I would actually say it makes sense for a new player to buy the game is when there's a deep discount of 70% or more, and even then only if they specifically get it to play total overhaul mods.

once this game was the ultimate experience, now  its being DLC salvaged slop by slop. You're not EA bro, you guys weren't always like this i don't know what happened to this company  also its been ten years, would you mind fixing the dogshid performance now perhaps? 
and another thing, if buying isn't owning then doing that "thing" isn't stealing so you guys know what to do don't pay for the transformation of this masterpiece into slop
the game does not need more updates just leave it alone, or at the very least fix the dam issues instead of creating useless stuff nearly no one wants

After achieving 100% achievements and spending well over 1,400 hours in the game, it feels like the perfect moment to leave a review.

This is a fantastic game to sink countless hours into, and therefore a great choice if you don't mind sacrificing your social life. Every campaign can play out differently, and by now most nations have received very solid focus trees that allow you to do almost anything you want in terms of map painting.

If you're interested in history, World War II, and grand strategy games, this is an easy recommendation. The game goes into great detail, and with the available DLC's and mods, you can pretty much do whatever you want.

All DLC's add a significant amount of content to the base game, and in my opinion, playing without them is not recommended. At this point, I would say all DLC's are worth having if you're interested in playing a wide variety of countries. However, I would recommend starting with the DLC subscription. For a relatively low price, you can test all features and later decide which DLC's you want to purchase permanently.

That said, there are still a few things that continue to frustrate me, especially while grinding achievements. This is most noticeable when playing minor nations. Many achievements require defeating the Allies, and the recent naval changes haven't made this any easier. The days of occasionally getting lucky and sneaking an invasion into Britain through a brief window of naval supremacy are essentially gone due to the new naval dominance system.

As a minor nation, you now often need a combination of luck and competent faction members (and don't count on Germany, because the AI has become pretty terrible at times) to gain enough naval dominance. You also need to act quickly, since the USA  joins the Allies in 1941.

For a large portion of the achievements, save-scumming or heavy use of cheesy strategies feels almost mandatory. This issue also carries over into normal campaigns, even when you're not hunting achievements. The game is great right up until the point where you need to invade Britain to force a peace deal. Of course, you can play on non-historical settings, but then you never really know what kind of world you're going to end up with.

Honestly, this is the only major criticism I can think of, and I don't see an easy solution. The mechanic itself is fairly logical, and historically speaking invading Britain was an incredibly difficult (if not impossible) task.

Still, I think the addition of a more widespread white peace mechanic would be a welcome change, similar to what already exists for certain countries. Especially for newer players, it would make campaigns easier to conclude and reduce the need to fight a world war all the way to the bitter end.

Screw Paradox's monitisation strategy. The prices of DLC are absurd. That wouldn't matter so much if the base game was serviceable, but feature creap makes the base game look like a skeleton. Unlike Rimworld or Crusader Kings 3 -- as far as I understand without owning it -- HOI4's base content is not enough to support the cost of DLC, or what mods can add. Unless you feel like you will get value out of the game as I have, I would not recommend it.

Buggy, empty, shallow and overly complicated, buy for like 7$ like i did or hate yourself. I used the dlc pass to see what was offered and unlike some paradox games, this is just a giant ripoff. Far less added with each dlc than should have been allowed and the game itself is just boring. You have very little control over anything including movement commands, your troops generally just do whatever they want in the general area you directed them. UI is atrocious and looks like a complete amateur made it for a school project. Don't be fooled by the descriptions, you wont be simulating much of anything here. :(

ON a final note, I have second hand embarassment for the dev that was in charge of programming movement commands. It's pretty much the most basic feature and it's pretty much Terrible.

I still love playing this game, but this negative review is for the atrocious no compromise no surrender dlc. Without it, you don't even have certain army doctrines to choose from, which its free to access before. Despite other dlc is also overpriced, I understand with that some provided flavor packs and some provided new mechanisms. Player can choose whether they want it or not, with this new dlc i can only access partial doctrines which is absurdity. Why not in the future do the same with special forces, only the one who buys the dlc can have paratroopers. This is a worrying new dlc marketing that do subtraction instead of application to serve its customer. I will not buy  new dlc and neither should u guys.

A true strategy game. This game encompasses all that strategy games need. A good replayability, tons upon tons of content, wide variety of tactics which the player can choose between. I have sank many hours into this game purely for how fun and addictive this game is. It truly allows you to think of alternate timelines in history and to act them out as they really happened. It is unfortunate that it seems the developers care a lot about money and put every big update behind a paywall; a dlc which has to be bought. Altogether, a really solid game.
Great Way to black out and find your self 5 Hours later 10/10 would recommend

I don’t even know why I bought this game. Since 2018, I haven’t played it a single time - all the hours Steam shows are just from using Idle Master. Why? Because I don’t have much time to play, and when a game demands commitment and several hours to learn its mechanics, I don’t want it to change a year later and force me to learn everything again just because I took a break and didn’t play for a long time. So knowing how Paradox makes their games, I have no desire even to try to play the game.

Just like Stellaris (a game I absolutely loved, but can’t play anymore for the same reasons, aside from the 60 hours I put in at release), this game is an endless early access.

And I don’t even want to start talking about the DLC.
No compromise, no surrender broke the navy and naval invasion which was one of the most fun parts of the game. :(

Only paradox game I don't recommend. This game uses complex mechanics that masquerade as strategic depth. But complexity is not identical to strategy. Chess is mechanically simple but strategically deep. HOI4 is mechanically complex but strategically shallow. The complexity can hide this fact upfront, and had I known I just wouldn't have played it. It's fine if your goal is to just role play ww2 scenarios. But if you are looking for a good strategy game, I'd suggest looking elsewhere.

Game's pretty good, great modding support aswell but paradox is too focused on milking a game that turns a decade old this year on an engine that is held together by hopes and dreams and it runs like it.
Not very fun and way to hard to learn for new players, also this game is $50 and there's still dlcs and stuff like holy scam.

This is a good game like Skyrim is good with mods. This purchase is basically just an engine you buy so you can download the free game that runs on it. Purchase when under 5 dollars, and never buy dlc unless it's relevant to the mod you like. Vanilla is basically garbage.

Hearts of Iron 4: the $50 game where all of the content is sold separately!

Paradox would rather lock fixes to core issues within the game behind a $20 DLC rather than try to update what already exists. The base game is so disgustingly neglected that the only way to squeeze any enjoyment from this game is to buy and play with the DLCs, costing you over $150 USD ON TOP OF THE $50 YOU ALREADY PAID!

In short: You will not regret your purchase if you like spreadsheet map games, are a larper, or hold little value for your time. 

In long: With HOI4 celebrating its tenth anniversary, and with myself having been around for seven of those years, I think it’s worth contemplating on a game that I genuinely enjoy despite it’s flaws and one that I keep coming back to even when I can’t rationalise why. I will divide this into several paragraphs that you can skip between as follows:

What is HOI4?
Difficulty
Performance
DLC Policy
The Modding Community

Hearts of Iron IV is a real-time 4X strategy game that takes place across the interbellum, bellum and postbellum of the Second World War. You commandeer a nation in this period and must develop new technologies, equip and structure armies, design and produce tanks, planes and fleets and guide your nation through a focus tree (railroaded missions that give you buffs and trigger events). The game has an immense number of systems that are interconnected and can be quite overwhelming when you’re getting started but you can get far enough by avoiding the majority of them. There is a fantastic number of spreadsheets of all forms in this game and I don’t doubt at some point you’ll have notepad alt-tabbed. 

There is a strong amount of content in the base game that has continually been expanded over the years with free updates. Almost every major country has a respectable amount of unique flavour content and there are a number of events that allow you a lot of opportunity to switch things up. Furthermore, even without DLC’s you are capable of changing your ideology and declaring war on whoever you want if fascist UK or communist USA are your thing. Most of the flavour content for alternative history paths for countries are locked behind DLC’s however, and without DLC’s a large number of countries are stuck with generic focus trees, which is quite boring. 

Compared to other Paradox games I would argue that HOI4 has a less pronounced difficulty curve than some of the other games. Usually within your first few games you will have a rudimentary enough understanding to compete against the AI and before long you will likely find little challenge in them. However, this is only true for the AI (who are not capable of using the majority of systems in this game competently nor are they able to counterplay against a wide variety of the cheese tactics and templates at your disposal). 

However, I did lie earlier. If you are playing multiplayer the difficulty curve goes parabolic. There are so many guides, theorycrafting and mathematics that go into playing the game optimally at higher levels that I cannot even delude myself into believing I would ever be able to understand it. Every minutia of the games systems that you can tinker with will have an optimal way to be employed to gain even the smallest of an advantage. There are countless guides and youtube videos that are released every patch that is an arms race to figure out the best meta, or to at least just grab a template from Bitt3rsteel and hope for the best. If you are a highly competitive person you will love this, there are many active multiplayer communities and many games and tournaments regularly hosted. Otherwise, stick to the AI or friends who aren’t that serious (the latter is very enjoyable).

Performance wise, irregardless of specs your game will likely begin to slow down as time goes on, which is more of an issue within the game's engine than anything else. This unfortunately manifests as the late-game drawing out incessantly long to the point where it is no longer fun to play, yet by this stage you most likely have already won or are so far ahead that you are guaranteed to. 

Paradox's monetisation scheme for the game is rightfully one of the most controversial elements of it, continually the DLC's are delivered half-baked and overpriced and very often do they lock crucial features behind them. In typical Paradox fashion there is a subscription available if you don’t want to shell out several hundred dollars for a game about green and red circles on a map upfront or there are less than scrupulous methods you can resort to. Regardless, it is true that the consistent updates and support for HOI4 would not be possible if it wasn’t for this continual monetisation. Otherwise, just buy the ones that you want when they are on sale, there are some much more important than others (again, this is Paradox).

By far the greatest enjoyment to be derived from this game in my opinion is through mods. The modding community for this game is a real marvel and something that almost everyone who plays makes use of. Want to play in the First World War as the Austro-Hungarians and save your empire? Want to play in the modern day and lead America through a civil war? Want to fight a globe-spanning German Empire as communist Britons after their victory in the First World War? Want to fight a globe-spanning Third Reich as Nixon after their victory in the Second World War? Want player-led piece deals so the UK stops making every Italian state a separate puppet? What if Hitler was an anime girl? What if Hirohito was an anime girl? What if FDR was an anime girl? What if- you get the point. If you want it, there is most likely a mod for it. If you don’t know you want it yet, you’ll see a mod for it. The MLP mod Equestria at War is also surprisingly good, try it out. Seriously. 

In conclusion, I’m surprised you’ve stayed around so long, thank you. HOI4 is a special game in that despite the community's universal apparent loathing of it (myself included), deep down we keep coming back. There's a certain proverbial itch that painting a map your colour scratches and there is a great satisfaction in getting a difficult achievement after a long run or in turning back the tide of battle to claw victory from the jaws of defeat.

Do I recommend this game? Yes, especially if it’s on sale. You’ll likely keep coming back to it for a long time after, even if you swear to never play it again. If I was to look at this game at an hour of enjoyment per dollar spent metric, it has probably been the best investment I have ever made.

Second, you can't back out of guarantees, this is incredibly odd as the British did this a lot in World War Two. Third, all the good stuff is locked behind DLCs, I've watched YouTubers do a play through and thought I want to do that but realize I don't have a 20$ DLC. Its a good game that really gets on my nerves, if you do not take losing well, don't play this, you loose a lot and for stupid reasons, I had a run where I was the German Empire and I guaranteed Poland and the Soviets invaded Poland and I couldn't do much as my army was still being prepared and I was on a two front war (France was communist) so do not get attached to your country and try to find a powerful ally to back you up

Not recommended as of 2026. 

Devs are constantly making dramatic gameplay changes that would require players to re-learn an already high-learning-curve game. Most of the strategies you can find online or developed by yourself through years of experience no longer works. Devs should have instead focused on fixing some 10 year-old bugs or meaningful content that does not break the game (like probably the last 10 country packs and expansions?).

Paradox games are the best, when the devs are done with them, and moved on to the next game.

A truly wonderful game. 
I spent hours playing it and never got bored. Over the years, not only have the numerous alternate-history scenarios increased, but unfortunately so has the number of DLCs. 
Nevertheless, I would still highly recommend buying this game, provided you’re both a keen strategy fan and a history buff.

And besides, giraffes are heartless creatures.
Amazing game a little complicated but easy once you get the hand of it.
Great game, steep learning curve, but definitely worth getting into. Takes the "one more turn" mentality from Civilization and crank it up by 11. Good luck lol
Cool game, I really like it, but the combat (especially with small country's) is pretty hard to learn, I highly recommend you to play it.

I've played this game for 250+ hours on Steam alone. Outside of Steam (let's just say I got a copy from a "friend"), I've probably sunk 500+ hours on it, maybe even more. I've been playing it since early 2024.

It is a fun game, a buggy, ♥♥♥♥♥♥♥♥ mess, but it is genuinely fun, real addictive. 9/10. Great strategy game. I love map painting, and definitely recommend playing it.

But folks, I can't recommend buying it. Why? The game really needs its DLCs. Without them, it's just an empty husk. You could buy the subscription, sure, but for real, you're better off getting a copy from a "friend" than paying every month for what feels like an unfinished product.

At 500 hours, I realized I didn't.
At 1,100 hours, I have accepted that nobody understands this game and we're all just moving little men around a map while pretending we know what the navy does

Not to mention how it plays with people with no dlc or only partial dlcs. If you want to buy dlc buy at at discount whenever they appear but most of my enjoyment from this game has come mods more than anything. The modding community is insane about what they can do and it's impressive but vanilla now seems likes it trying to match the mods in community to which it simply can't.

Admittedly I still find much of the game overwhelming at times and often get fatigue from longer sessions. I do still prefer the Civilization series, but this game is still good fun.

I bought Hearts of Iron IV thinking it would be a fun little strategy game to play for a few hours.

431.4 hours later, I no longer measure time in days or weeks. I measure it in focuses, production queues, and failed invasions of the Soviet Union.

At first I was a normal person. Then I started saying things like "just one more campaign" at 11 PM. Suddenly it was 4 AM and I was calculating tank division templates instead of sleeping.

My friends stopped hearing from me. My family thought I had disappeared. In reality, I was spending three hours deciding whether to build civilian factories or military factories.

The worst part is that I slowly became Napoleon. Every map I see now makes me think about supply lines. Every conversation becomes a discussion about logistics. I cannot look at Europe without planning an invasion.

Would I recommend HOI4?

Absolutely, now I wait for the board game to arrive so I can force any being who steps into my house play with me.

Thank you Paradox

This game is perfect for people that love ww2 games and people who love strategic games the multiplayer is a little chaotic but can be intense sometimes the first times you play it will seem confusing but after a few sessions you start having a lot of fun would recommend!
